               Relations
              A relation from a set A to a set B is a subset of A x B, that is, a set of ordered pairs (a, b)
              such that a E A and b E R If R is a relation, one often writes aR b instead of (a, b) E R.
                  A relation from A to A is also called a (binary) relation in A. As an example of a relation
              in IR, consider the "less than" relation consisting of all ordered pairs of real numbers (x, y)
              withx < y.
                  The domain of a relation R from A to B is the set
                  dom(R) = {a EA: (a, b) ER for some bin B} = {a EA: aRb for some bin B}
              of possible first elements, whereas the range of R is the set
                 range(R) = {b E B : (a, b) E R for some a in A}= {b E B : aRb for some a in A)
              of possible second elements in the set R of ordered pairs. The inverse of a relation R from
              A to Bis the relation R- 1 from B to A given by R- 1 = {(b, a) E B x A: (a, b) E R}.
                  If R is a relation from A to B and S is a relation from B to C, we define the composition
              So R of R and Sas the set of all (a, c) in A x C such that there is an element bin B with
              a Rb and bSc, So R is then a relation from A to C.
                  A relation R in X is reflexive if x Rx for all x in X, It is transitive if x Ry and y R z
              imply xRz, it is symmetric if xRy implies yRx, it is anti-symmetric if xRy and yRx
              imply x = y, and it is complete if for all x and yin X at least one of xR y or yRx holds.
              A partial ordering in X is a relation in X that is reflexive, transitive, and anti-symmetric.
              If a partial ordering is complete, it is called a linear (or total) ordering. The relation :::: in
              IR is a linear ordering.
                  For n ~ 2, the less-than-or-equal-to relation ~ in 1R 11 is defined by (x 1 , . , . , Xn) ~
              (Yi, , .. , y11 )*  Xk :'.:: Yk for k = 1, .. , , n. The symbol ::':: is then usually taken to mean
              ":£, but not = ", (Some authors also use < in this sense when comparing vectors. Cau-
              tion is necessary!) "I11ere is also a strict inequality relation    «,                             «
                                                                                         given by (x 1 , , , , , Xn)
              (y1, ... , y 11 )* Xk < Yk for all k = l, .. ,, n. The relation c'£ is a partial ordering in !Rn.
                  An equivalence relation in X is a relation that is reflexive, transitive, and symmetric.
              If R is an equivalence relation in X, then R induces a partition of X into pairwise disjoint
              equivalence classes [x] = {y E X : y Rx}. The union of all these equivalence classes is X ·


               Functions
              A function (also called a mapping, map, or transformation) f : X --+ Y from a set X to a
              set Y is a rule that assigns exactly one element y = .f (x) in Y to each x in X. In set-theoretic
              te1ms f is a relation from X to Y such that:
                                        SECTION A.1      /   SETS AND FUNCTIONS            529

   (1) dom(f) =

   (2) for each x in X there is exactly one y in Y such that x f y.

Thus f is "single-valued'" and "operates on" every x in X. One usually writes f(x)     y   =
instead of x f y. The set X is the domain and Y is the codomain of f. TI1e range of f is
the same as the range of f considered as a relation:

         range(!)= {y E f : y = f (x) for at least one x in X} = [f (x) : x EX}

The last formulation is an example of a somewhat sloppy notation that is often used when
the meaning is clear from the context The graph of f is the set

                         graph(!)= {(x, y) EX x Y: y         = f(x)}
This is of course the same as the relation f as defined in the previous subsection.
   If f(x) = y, one also writes f : x 1-+ y. The squaring function s : ~ --'r IR, for
example, can then be wTitten as s : x 1-+ x 2 . Thus, r+ indicates the effect of the function
on an element of the domain. If the domain A off : A --,, B is a subset of a set         it is
sometimes convenient to write f : A s; X -+ B, and rnaybe even f : A s; X --;,. B s::; Y if
B is a subset of Y,
   If f : A --;,. Bis a function and S s;       the restriction off to Sis the function f Is
defined by f ls(x) = f (x) for every x in S.
    A function f . A --'r B is "one-to-one" or injective if f (a) f    f (a') whenever a f a',
i.e. if f always maps distinct points in A to distinct poinls in B.
   If the range off : A -+ B is all of B, then f is called su.rjective or onto, and f is said
to map A onto B.
   When f : A --'r B is both injective and surjective (i.e. when it is both one-to-one and
onto), it is called bijective.
   If f : A --'r B is injective, then f has an inverse function 1- 1 : range(!) --;,. A, defined
by f- 1 (b) = a ·¢:> b = f(a). (Considered as a relation, f always has an inverse with
range(!) as its don:rn.in, but this inverse relation is afunction only when f is injective.)

      A.2     Least Upper Bound Principle
              The real number system is fully characterized by a rather small number of axioms. The
              usual algebraic rules and the rules for inequalities are all well known. Here we consider
              briefly only the so-called least upper bound principle. An understanding of this principle is
              crucial for many of the arguments in this book. The need for this principle can be illustrated
              by the problem of determining the area of a circle. We know how to calculate the area of
              plane regions bounded by straight lines. Figure 1 shows a sequence of regular polygons
              inscribed in a circle. For n :::: 3, let An be the area of a regular n-sided polygon inscribed
              in the circle. Thus, A3 is the area of an equilateral triangle, A4 is the area of a square, As is
              the area of a regular pentagon, and so on.
                               SECTION A.2       /     LEAST UPPER BOUND PRINCIPLE            531




                                               Figure 1


    The area of the circle must be greater than each of the numbers A3, A4, As, .... On the
    other hand it seems clear that by choosing n sufficiently large, we can make the difference
    between the area of the circle and the area of an inscribed regular n-gon as small as we
    please. We now define the area of the circle as the smallest number greater than or equal
    to each of the numbers A3, A4, As, . . . . This definition makes sense only because the
    existence of such a number is a basic property of the set of real numbers, called the principle
    of least upper bound.
        Recall that a set S of real numbers is bounded above if there exists a real number b such
    that b :::: x for all x in S. Any such number bis called an upper bound for S. A set that is
    bounded above has many upper bounds. A least upper bound for the set S is a number b*
    that is an upper bound for Sand is such that b* ::: b for every upper bound b,
        The existence of a least upper bound is a basic and non-tlivial property of the real
    number system.




    Any nonempty set of real numbers that is bounded above has a least upper bound.            (1)




    A set Scan have at most one least upper bound, because if bj and      are both least upper
    bounds for S, then bf :S b2and b2 ::: bj, and thus b1= b't The least upper bound b* of S
    is often called the supremum of S. We write b* = sup Sor b* = supxES x.


                                        s
                        --~,~--~-;;;:"'---------+-----+
                                                        t        h
                                                     b* = sups

                        Figure 2 Any b :::: b' is an upper bound for S, but
                        b"' = sup S is the unique least upper bound for S.


l       The set S = (0, 5), consil,ting of all x such that 0 < x < 5, has many upper bounds,
    including 100, 6.73, and 5. Clearly, no number smaller than 5 can be an upper bound., so 5
    is the least upper bound. Tims sup S = 5.
        The set T = (x :      < 2] = (-,Ji, .,/2) has many upper bounds, which include 9, 2,
    and ./2. Clearly, no number smaller than .-./2 can be an upper bound of T, and so ,fi is the
    least upper bound, sup T = .j2,
532   APPENDIX A     /   SETS, COMPLETENESS, AI\ID CONVERGENCE


             A set S is bounded below if there exists a real number a such that x 2: a for all x in S. The
             number a is called a lower bound for S. A set S that is bounded below has a greatest lower
             bound a'", with lhe property a* ~ x for all x in S, and a* 2: a for all lower bounds a. The
             number a* is called the infinmm of Sand we write a* = inf Sor a* = infxES x. Thus,

                          sup S = the least number greater than or equal to all numbers in S                       (2)
                           inf S   = the greatest number less than or equal to all numbers in S                    (3)

             If S is not bounded below, we write inf S = -oo. If S is not bounded above, we write
             sups= 00.
                Every real number is an upper bound for 0, the empty set. Therefore 0 has no least upper
             bound, and by convention we write sup 0 = -oo. Similarly, inf 0 = oo.
                The following characterization of the supremum is easy to prove:




             Let S be a set of real numbers and b* a real number. Then sup S = b* if and only
             if the following two conditions are satisfied:
              (a) x ~ b':, for all x in S.
              (b) For each r; > 0 there exists an x in S such that x > b* - £.



             NOTE 1 The existence of a supremurn of a set bounded above may seem to be "intuitively
             evident": Staii with a nonempty set S of real numbers and an upper bound b for S, as in
             Fig. 2. Then move b to the left until it is "stopped" by the set S. The number bis still an
             upper bound for S, and it is the least of all the upper bounds.
                 The least upper bound principle is a non-trivial property of the real number system. To
             appreciate this, observe that the principle does not hold within the set (IJ of rational numbers.
             For instance, let S be the set of all rational numbers r such that r 2 < 2. Within the rational
             number system, the set S has no least upper bound. All rational numbers larger than or equal
             to .Ji are upper bounds for S, but there is not a smallest one among these numbers because
             .,/2 is iffational (See Problem 2.)

A.3        Sequences                    Real Numbers
           A sequence can be viewed as a function k 1--+ x(k) with the set N = {], 2, 3, ... } of all
           positive integers as its domain. The terms x(1), x(2), x(3), ... , x(k), ... of the sequence
           are usually denoted by using subscripts: x1, x2, X3, ... , xk, .... We shall use the notation
           {xdk=l' or simply {xk h, or even just {xk}, to indicate an arbitrary sequence of real numbers.
              A sequence [xd of real numbers is said to be
           (a) increasing (or nondecreasing) if x1c ::: Xk+J fork = l, 2, ... ;
           (b) strictly increasing if x1c < x1c+1 fork= l, 2, ... ;
           (c) decreasing (or nonincreasing) if Xk ::: Xk+ 1 for k = 1, 2, ... ;
           (d) strictly decreasing if Xk > Xk+i fork = 1, 2, ....
           A sequence that is increasing or decreasing is called monotone.

            A sequence {xi<} is bounded if there exists a number M such that lxkl:::. M for all k = l,

Rules for Handling Convergent Sequences
Suppose that {xk) converges to x and (y1c] converges to y as k -+ oo. For k sufficiently
large, Xk is close to x and Yk is close toy. Then Xk + Yk must be close to x + y, and it is
therefore reasonable to believe that xk + Yk -+ x + y as k -> oo. Conesponding results
hold for subtraction, multiplication, and division. In fact, we have the following result:




Suppose that the sequences {x1c} and [Yk} converge to x and y, respectively. Then:
(a) lim1c-,.oo(x1c   + Yk) = x + Y
(b) limk-+oo(X1c - Yk) = X - Y
(c) lim1c--, 00 (xk Y1c) = x · Y
(b) limk-+oo(x,dyk) = x/y, assuming that Yk f= 0 for all k and y i 0.

convergent, there exists a number N1 such that lx1c - x I < s /2 for all k > Ni. In the same
way there exists a number N2 such that IYk - YI < s/2 for all k > N2. Let N be the greater
of the two numbers N1 and N2. Then fork >

But this means that limk(Xk +     = x + y = lim1c Xk + limk Yk·
   The statement in (b) is proved in the same way. Proving (c) and (d) requires more
complicated arguments to show that lx1cYk -xyl and lx,dy1c -x /yl are less than an arbitrary

Cauchy Sequences
The definition (1) of a convergent sequence involves the specific value of the limit. If this
1imit is unknown, or inconvenient to calculate, the definition is not very useful because one
cannot test all numbers to see if they meet the criterion. An important alternative necessary
and sufficient condition for convergence is based on the following concept:




A sequence {x1c) of real numbers is called a Cauchy sequence if for every s > 0,
there exists a natural number N such that                                                    (2)

                  lx 11 - x,,, I < £   for all n > N and all m > N



Note that the terms of a Cauchy sequence will eventually be close together, which need
not be the case for an arbitrary sequence. In particular, the sequence {xd = {(-1/),
whose terms are alternatively -1 and l, is clearly not a Cauchy sequence. But the sequence
{yk} ={(-½/}is.
   AU the terms of a convergent sequence eventually cluster around the limit, so the se-
quence is a Cauchy sequence. The converse is also true-that is, every Cauchy sequence is
convergent:




A sequence is convergent if and only if it is a Cauchy sequence.



         To prove the "only if" part, suppose that {xd converges to x. Given & > 0, choose
a natural number N such that lxk - x I < s /2 for all k > N. Then fork > N and m > N,

       lxk - Xm I = I(xk - x) + (x - Xm) I S lx1.: - x I + Ix - Xm I < £ /2 + £ /2 = &

Therefore {xk] is a Cauchy sequence.
    To prove the "if'., part, suppose {x1c} is a Cauchy sequence. We first show that the sequence
is bounded. By the Cauchy property, there is a number M such that lxk-XM I < l fork > M.
This means that all points Xk with k > M have a distance from XM that is less than 1.
Moreover, the finite set {x1, x2, ... , XM-d is surely bounded. Hence, {xd is bounded.
    By TheoremA.3.4, therefore, the sequence{,,}} has a convergent subsequence {Xk; }. Let
x = lim1 Xk;, Because {xk} is a Cauchy sequence, for every£ > 0 there is a natural number
N such that jx 11 - x 111 I < s /2 for n > N and m > N. Moreover, if J is sufficiently large,
lxk; - xi< s/2 for all j > J. Then fork> N and j > max{N, J},



Hence, Xk -+ x ask -+ oo.                                                                      II

               Thus, for any E > 0, if we choose n > 1/s, then lxm - x 11 I < E for all m > n. This proves

            On lim inf and lim sup of Functions
            This section concludes by defining some limit concepts that are needed in connection with infinite
            horizon dynamic economic models. TI1ey also help to understand the definitions of upper and lower
            hemicontinuous correspondences in Section 14, 1.
                Recall first the standard definition of a lirnit of a function of several variables: Let f be a function
            defined on a set M in IR", and suppose that x 0 E cl(M), the closure of M. We say that f (x)
                 SEC T I O N A . 4 /         I N FI M U M A N D SU PREM U M O F FU N CT I O 1\1 S                  543

converges to the number A as x tends to x 0 , if for each e > 0, there exists a number 8 > 0 such that
llf(x) - f(i/l)II < e for all x in M with x f x 0 and llx - x 0 11 < 8.
    Next we define the upper and lower limits off at x 0 as

                    liminf j(x) = lim(inf{ .f(x): x E B(x 0 ; r) n M, x f x 0 })                                   (10)
                      x-+xO              r--P-0

                    lim sup f(x) = lim(sup{ f(x). x E B(x0· r) n M, x =f=. x0 })                                   (11)
                     x--.-xo             r-,-o


Just as for sequences, we often write ]irn and Jim for Jim inf and Jim sup. With these definitions we
obtain the following rules, which are based on the cmTesponding rules for inf and sup:

       lim (f (x) + g(x)) ::: Jim f (x)           + Jim g(x)                 (if the right-hand side is defined)   (12)
      X--+XO                   K--+XO               X-rXO

Note that if lirn,-+,o f (x) exists at a point x 0 , then limx-Ho f (x) = limx-+xo f (x) = lim,--,.xa f(x).
Conversely, if limx~,xo f (x) = limx-+xa f (x), then lim,~xo f (x) exists and is equal to both.
   A function f is called upper semicontirrm:ms at a point x 0 in M if limx--;-,o f (x) ::: f(x 0 ). The
function f is called lower semkontinuous at x 0 if limH,o f (x) ::: f ("x 0). This definition allows a
generalization of the extreme value theorem which says that, if K £; ~" is a nonempty compact set,
and if the function f is upper semicontinuous, the f has a maximum point in the set K. If f is lower
semicontinuous, the f has a minimum point in K.

      M       any phenomena appear to repeat themselves with predictable regularity. Examples are
               alternating electric currents in physics, heartbeat and respiration in physiology, and sea-
      sonal variations in economics such as increased demand for heating fuel and warm clothing
      in winter, as opposed to air-conditioning and cool clothing in summer. Many economists have
      also looked for regular periodic patterns in macroeconomic variables like national output or in-
      terest rates. To describe such phenomena mathematically, one possibility is to use trigonometric
      functions, which are briefly reviewed in Sections B.1 and B.2. The final Section B.3 gives a brief
      introduction to complex numbers.


8.1   Basic Definitions and Results
      Consider the circle in Fig. 1 with radius 1 and centre at the origin in the u v-plane.




                                                                    A= (1, 0)




                                        Figure 1 sinx = v and cosx = u


      Let A be the point on the circle with coordinates ( l, 0), and let Px be t11e point on the circle
      for which the arc length between A and       is x. The point A is Po, of course.
          The arc x is measured with the same unit of length as the radius. Because the radius of
      the circle is r = 1, the circumference equals 2nr = 211:. If x = n:/2, we go one-quarter
546   A PP E1\1 D I X B /   TR I G O N O M ETR I C FU N C TI O 1\1 S


                 of the way round the circle in an anticlockwise direction to arrive at the point Prr/2, which
                 has coordinates      v) = (0, 1). For Pn, we go halfway round to the point with coordinates
                 (u,v) = (-l,0);forP3rr;2,weget(u,v) = (0,-l);forPo = P2rr,wehave(u,v) = (1,0);
                 and so on. For the point l'x shown in Fig. 1, where x N 1.1, we have u N 0.45 and v N 0.9,
                 If xis negative, we go a distance -x clockwise around the circle.
                     In general, as x increases, P,. moves round the unit circle, and the values of u and v
                 oscillate up and down. They repeat themselves as Px passes through points where it has
                 been before. In particulax, x, x ± 2n, x ± 4n, and so on, all define the same point on the
                 circle. TI1us, Px = Px+2nn for n = ±1, ±2, .... This procedure maps each real number x
                 to a point /\ with coordinates (u, v).

                                   The sine function is the rule that maps x to the number v.

                                   The cosine function is the rule that maps x to the number u.

                 It is standard to abbreviate sine to sin and cosine to cos. So, referring to Fig. 1, we have

                                                   sinx = v            and       cosx = u                            (1)

                 The circle in Fig. l has the equation u 2 + v2 = L This implies the important relationship:

                                                         (sinx) 2 + (cosx)2 = 1                                      (2)

                 The domains of the functions sin and cos are the set of all real numbers. The range of each
                 is the closed interval [-1, l]. Note also that a small change in x will change the point P,
                 only slightly, so the coordinates u and v will also change only slightly, and v = sin x and
                 u = cos x are both continuous functions of x. (In fact, from Fig. 1, we see that a given
                 change in x causes changes in u and v that are smaller in absolute value.)
                     If xis any number such that cos x i= 0, we define the tangent function by simply dividing
                 sin x by cos x. It is standard to abbreviate tangent to tan, so that

                                                    sinx
                                             tanx = - -                (provided that cos x i= 0)                    (3)
                                                    cosx

                 The cotangent function, abbreviated cot, is defined by cot x = cos x / sin x, for all x with
                 sin x i= 0. It is clear that cot x = I/ tan x when tan x and cot x are both defined.
                     Note that it is common practice to write sin 2 x for (sin x )2, cos 2 x for (cos x ) 2 , and tan 2 x
                 for (tan x ) 2 . Similar notation is also used for higher powers of the trigonometric functions.
                 For example, cos 3 x = (cos x )3 .



                 Measuring Angles in Radians
                 In trigonometry, it is common to define the sine, cosine, and tangent as functions of the
                 angle, which is often measured in degrees. Figure 1 shows how the arc length x can be used
                 instead to measure the angle AO Px. TI1en it is said that the angle is measured in radians.
                 In elementary geometry it is common practice to operate with degrees, so one must know
                             SECT I ON B . 1 /     BAS I C D EFI N IT I ON S AN D RES ULT S                          54 7

how to convert degrees into radians and vice versa. In fact, 360° = 2n radians because
when x = 2n, the line O    has rotated through 360°. So we have the following:


              1° = (--.re. ) radians R:J O.017 radians,    l radian= ( 18

The degree scale for measuring angles is built on an arbitrary choice of unit in that the
complete circle is divided into 360°. This corresponds to the ancient Babylonian calendar
that divided the year into 360       From a mathematical point of view, the radian scale is
the most natural one for measuring angles. The reason is that calculus formulas are simpler
when angles are measured in radians rather than in degrees.
    There is no method for finding exact numerical values of the trigonometric functions
in the general case. Programs to calculate numerical approximations arc available on most
calculators.

Trigonometric Formulas
There is a plethora of trigonometric formulas that have pestered high school 8tudents (and
their parents) for generations. Nevertheless, the following formula is particularly useful:

                              cos(x + y) = cosxcosy- sinxsiny                                 (8)

(For a proof, see Problem 13.) By using this basic equation, similar fonnulas for cos(x - y),
sin(x + y), and sin(x - y) are quite easy to prove (see Problems 3 and 4).


Transformed Trigonometric Functions
We have discussed some important properties of the three basic trigonometric functions: sin,
cos, and tan. In economics, they are mainly used Jn connection with periodic phenomena.
Usually transformations of the functions must be used.
    So far we have seen that y = sin x is periodic with period 2n:. The graph of the function
shows a wavelike curve that is said to have period ( or wavelength) 2n:. If, instead, we
represent graphically the function given by y = sin(x/2), we still get a wavelike curve, but
the period is now twice as long, namely 4n. The reason is that when x increases from x0 to
xo + 4.rr, then x /2 increases from xo/2 to xo/2 + 21(, so sin(x /2) is peliodic with period 4n.
More generally, y = sin(ax) is periodic with period 2n / a, because as x increases by 2n / a,
so ax increases by 2rr. The value of y = sin(ax) will still oscillate between -1 and 1, and
we say that the runplitude is equal to 1. To get a periodic function with amplitude A, just
put y = A sin ax, which varies between -A and A. Hence,

                       y = A sin(ax)        has period 2n /a and amplitude A

The reciprocal, a/2n, of the peiiod is called the frequency. It is the number of oscillations
per radian.
                              )'


                                                  I<---- 2n/a - - - 1

B.3 Complex Numbers
   The concept of number can be extended from natural numbers l, 2, ... via integer:-: (0, ± 1,
   ±2, ... ), and then rationals (such as L414 or 22/7) to real numbers (including ~, e,
   and n). Each of these extensions expands the set of equations that have solutions, Now,
   simple quadratic equations like x 2 + 1 = 0 and x 2 + 4x + 8 = 0 have no solution within
   the real number              By introducing complex numbers, however, all quadratic equations
   become soluble. In              within the complex number system, any polynornial equation
                                  +
   xn + a 11 -1x 11 - 1 + , , + a 1x ao = 0 has solutions.
      The standard formula for solving the equation x 2 + 4x + 8 = 0 yields the expressions
   - 2 + ,,/=4 and --2 ,-   ,;=;r     So far, we have not given any meaning to these expressions,
   Bui if we take the liberty of writing R        = AR = 2,J=T, we obtain the "solutions"

                                                 and       - 2 - 2,J"_:-:f

   Here -2 and 2 arc well-known numbers, but .J=T is not. By pretending that A               is a
   number i whose square is -1, however, we make i a solution of the equation i 2 = - I.
       By treating these expressions as if they satisfy the usual algebraic rules, with the addi-
   tional provision that ,,/=1,J=T means -1, expressions of the type a+ b,A can be used
   to solve all quadratic equation,;, even those without real roots.
       The symbol ~:l' can only be given a meaning in an extended system of "numbers",
   which we call complex numbers. Mathematical formalism regards them as 2-vectors (a, b)
   equipped with the standard addition mle, but with a new multiplication rule.
       Informally, instead of writing (a, b), we usually write this complex number as a+ bi,
   where a and bare real numbers and i '"•"v,w ...., the (so far undefined) symbol ,,/=T Think
   of i as a symbol that simply identifies which is the second component in the complex number.
   The real number a is called the real part, and the real number bis called the imaginary part
   of the complex number (a, b). The operations of addition, subtraction and multiplication
   are defined by

                          (a+ bi)+ (c + di) = (a+ c) + (b + d)i                                  (l)
                          (a+ bi) - (c + di) = (a - c) + (b - d)i                                (2)
                             (a+ bi)(c + di) = (ac - bd) +(ad+ bc)i                              (3)

   respectively. Formally, rule (1) should be written in the fom1 (a, b)+ (c, d) = (a+b, c+d),
   and rule (3) should be written as (a, b)(c, d) = (ac-bd, ad +be). What makes the informal
   expressions a +bi attractive is the fact that (3) is what results if we perform the multiplication
   (a + bi)(c + di) according to the usual algebraic rules, thus obtaining the expression
   ac +(ad+ bc)i + bdi 2 , and then finally replace i 2 by -1. When multiplying complex
   numbers in practice, we usually perform the computation this way, rather than using rule
   (3) directly. The complex number (I, 0) is a "unit" in the sense that (1, O)(a, b) = (a, b).
       The way in which we divide two complex numbers can be motivated by the following
   calculations:

                     a+bi        (a + bi)(c - di)       (ac + bd) + (be - ad)i
                     C +di       (c + di)(c - di)                   c2 +d2
556   APPENDIX B /    TRIGONOMETRIC FUNCTIONS


             The division is defined when c 2 + d 2 =/= 0. The formal rule is this:

                                              (a, b) = (ac + bd be - ad)
                                              (c, d)       c 2 + d 2 ' c2 + d2


             In particular, the inverse of c + di is 1/(c + di) = (c - di)/(c 2 + d 2 ). Of course, we have
             to check that (c - di)/(c 2 + d 2 ) deserves the name inverse-that is, we have to check that
             (c + di)[(c - di)/(c 2 + d 2 )] = 1 + 0i, which indeed is the case.
                 Consider now the problem of giving a meaning to the symbol i = ,J=T. We formally treat
             this as the problem of finding a complex number (a, b) with the property that (a, b)(a, b) =
             (-1, 0). It is easy to see that there are two such complex numbers, (0, 1) and (0, -1),
             and we choose (0, 1) as our i. Then we can formally interpret the symbol a + bi as being
             (a, O)(l, 0) + (b, 0)(0, 1), where we simply omit (l, 0) and write a and b instead of (a, 0)
             and (b, 0).
                 It is common practice to denote complex numbers by single letters near the end of the
             alphabet, such as z = x + yi or w = u +vi. Two complex numbers, written in this manner,
             are equal iff both their real and their imaginary parts are equal-that is, z = w iff x = u and
             y = v. If the imaginary prut of a complex number is 0, we let x + Oi = x. In fact, complex.
             numbers of the form x + Oi behave just like the corresponding real numbers with respect
             to addition and multiplication. In particular, the numbers O (= 0 + Oi) and 1 ( = 1 + Oi)
             obey the same algebraic rules whether we regard them as complex or as real numbers.
             Furthennore, (x, 0)(u, v) = (x + 0i)(u +vi)= (xu, xv)= x(u, v), where, for once, we
             revert to ordinary vector algebra in the last expression, and write the product of a scalar and
             a vector.

                     (a) z + w = (3 + 4i) + (2 - Si) = 5 - i
                     (b) zw = (3 + 4i)(2 - Si)= 6 - 15/ + 8i - 20i 2 = 26 - 7i
                            z    3 +4i       (3 + 4i)(2 + 5i)       6 + 15i + Si -- 20       -14+23i
                     (c)
                           w     2 - 5i      (2 - 5i)(2 + 5i)             4+25                    29

                Prove that if z. is a complex number where z 2 is real and nonnegative, then 2 is real.

                           If z = x + iy, then 2 2 = (x + iy) 2 = x 2 + 2xyi + (iy) 2 = x 2 - y 2 + 2xyi.
             Thus z2 is real only if xy   = 0. Then either x = 0 or y = 0 (or both). Requiring 2 2 2: 0
             implies that x 2 ::C:. y2, so that y, at least, must be 0. But then z = x, so z is real.



             Trigonometric Form of Complex Numbers
             Each complex number z = x + yi = (x, y) can be represented by a point in the plane.
             Figure 1 shows how to represent the three particular complex numbers i, -i, and 3 + 2i ·
                                                     SECTION B.3      /   COMPLEX NUMBERS                    557

   Not surprisingly, the plane representing complex numbers is called the complex plane.
The horizontal       representing numbers of the form x + Oi, is called the real axis, and
the vertical     representing numbers of the form O + yi, is called the imaginary axis.

                 Imaginary                                                Imaginary
                   axis                                                     axis

       Figure 1 The complex plane.                              Figure 2. Polar coordinates.


Instead of representing a complex number z = x + yi by the pair (x, y), we could use polar
coordinates. As illustrated in Fig. 2, let 0 be the angle (measured in radians) between the
positive real axis and the vector from the origin to the point (x, y), and let r be the distance
from the origin to the same point. Then x = r cos 8 and y = r sine, so


                                        z = x + yi = r(cos 8 + i sin 0)                                          (4)


The last expression is the trigonometric (or iJOlar) form of the complex number z. The
angle 0 is called the argmnent of the complex number z. :Note that the distance from the
origin to the point (x,                        J
                           is r = x 2 + y 2 . This is called the modulus of the complex
number, denoted by lzl. Hence,


                              lzl = Jx 2 + y 2      is the modulus of z = x + yi                                 (5)


If z = x +     then the complex conjugate of z is donned as z = x -                                We see that
zz = x 2 + y2 = lzi 2 , where lzl is the modulus of z.
   Multiplication and division of complex numbers have neat geometric interpretations if
we represent the numbers in trigonometric form. Indeed, applying (3) gives




because (B.1.8) and Problem B. 1 A imply that cos(01              +
                                                           02) = cos 81 cos 02 - sin 81 sin 82
and sin(01 + 82)    =
                   sin 01 cos 82 + cos Bt sin 82. Thus, the product of two complex numbers
is that complex number 1,vhose modulus is the product of the moduli of the two factors, and
whose argument is the sum of the arguments.
   Similarly, we can show that

                               (cos 0 + i sin e)3 = (cos e + i sin r9) 2 (cos e + i sin 0)
                                                  = (cos 20 + i sin 20)(cos 0 + i sin 0) = cos 30 + i sin 30

               By induction, we find a famous result, valid for all n = 1, 2, 3, ... ·

                                    ( cos 0 + i sin 0   t = cos n0 + i sin n 0      (de Moivre's formula)            (8)


               Vectors with Complex Components
               Sometimes we need to consider vectors (and matrices) with complex elements. We add and
               multiply with scalars (which can be complex numbers) in the obvious way, and the usual
               algebraic rules still hold. If z is a vector with complex components z1, z2, ... , z11 , we let z
               denote the vector with components z1, z2, ... , z11 , where Zj is the complex conjugate of Zf.
               Then the inner product of z and z is

                                                                                                                     (9)
               MOTE       This has been a very brief introduction to complex numbers. The need to extend
               the real number system arose in the sixteenth century when various Italian mathematicians
               derived analytic formulas for the solution to algebraic equations of degree 2, 3, and 4. For a
               long time, the complex numbers were regarded as "imaginary", mystical objects. Not any
               more. Actually, the extension of the number concept from the real numbers to the complex
               numbers is motivated by the same concern as the extension from the rationals to the reals.
               In both cases, we want certain equations to have solutions.
                   Nowadays complex numbers axe indispensable in mathematics. Modern science just
               could not do without them. However, they do not play a very large role in economics.
               In this book, they allow a convenient description of the solutions to some higher-order
               difference and differential equations. That also makes them useful in stating results on the
               stability of solutions to differential equations.

         aS + bT is convex.

 9. The Lagrangian is£ = x'Ax - J-.(x'x - !) . In vector notation,£~ = 2Ax - 2'Ax = 0, or(*) Ax= AX (see
    Note 2.3.4), so 1c is an eigenvalue of A, with x as an eigenvector. Multiplying (*) from the left by x' gives
    x' Ax = x' ('Ax) = 'Ax'x = 'A, because x'x = 1. We conclude that the maximum value of x' Ax subject to x'x = 1
    must be the largest eigenvalue of A, whereas the minimum value must be the smallest eigenvalue. Both a maximum
    and a minimum value exist by the extreme value theorem.

   yields an increasing subsequence of {xd.
